perturbation.original_text: 5. COMPANY shall complete its evaluation within the period expiring on the date six (6) months after the date COMPANY receives the Confidential Information from ROCHESTER.
perturbation.changed_text: 5. COMPANY should aim to complete its evaluation within a period that generally aligns with six (6) months after the date COMPANY receives the Confidential Information from ROCHESTER.
perturbation.explanation: The original text sets a firm deadline of six months for the evaluation. The revised text replaces 'shall' with 'should aim,' and introduces vagueness with 'generally aligns,' making the deadline non-binding and open to interpretation, potentially leading to delays and disputes about the evaluation period.
perturbation.law_explanation: New York UCC § 2-309 requires that where a contract provides for successive performances but is indefinite in duration it is valid for a reasonable time. The modified text, by making the six-month evaluation period non-binding, introduces indefiniteness. The obligation to complete the evaluation within six months is now only a suggestion and because of this, a court could be required to decide what is a reasonable time, leading to additional burden on ROCHESTER to enforce and prove their case.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: New York Consolidated Laws, Uniform Commercial Code - UCC § 2-309 | FindLaw New York Consolidated Laws, Uniform Commercial Code - UCC § 2-309. Absence of Specific Time Provisions; Notice of Termination Current as of January 01, 2024 | Updated by FindLaw Staff (1) The time for shipment or delivery or any other action under a contract if not provided in this Article or agreed upon shall be a reasonable time. (2) Where the contract provides for successive performances but is indefinite in duration it is valid for a reasonable time but unless otherwise agreed may be terminated at any time by either party. (3) Termination of a contract by one party except on the happening of an agreed event requires that reasonable notification be received by the other party and an agreement dispensing with notification is invalid if its operation would be unconscionable. perturbation.justification: The original contract specifies a firm deadline of six months using "shall," while the changed text uses "should aim" and "generally aligns," making the deadline non-binding. This contradicts New York UCC § 2-309, which states that if a contract doesn't specify a time for performance, a "reasonable time" applies. By weakening the six-month deadline to a non-binding suggestion, the contract introduces indefiniteness regarding the completion of the evaluation, thus potentially requiring a court to determine what constitutes a 'reasonable time,' which is against what was initially agreed. The FindLaw snippet (New York Consolidated Laws, Uniform Commercial Code - UCC § 2-309) explicitly mentions that 'The time for shipment or delivery or any other action under a contract if not provided in this Article or agreed upon shall be a reasonable time.'

perturbation.original_text: 6. At the end of the Evaluation Period, COMPANY shall upon request of ROCHESTER, return to ROCHESTER all Confidential Information in its files to monitor its obligations under this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: 6. Following the Evaluation Period, COMPANY will consider ROCHESTER's request to return the Confidential Information in its files as appropriate.
perturbation.explanation: The original text clearly states that the company *shall* return the confidential information upon request. The changed text makes the return of the information conditional by using the phrase 'will consider ROCHESTER's request', which introduces ambiguity and makes the obligation subject to the COMPANY's discretion, undermining ROCHESTER's control over its confidential information.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York Civil Practice Law and Rules (CPLR) § 3101
perturbation.law_explanation: New York CPLR § 3101 provides for full disclosure of all matter material and necessary in the prosecution or defense of an action, regardless of the burden of proof. By making the return of confidential information discretionary, the modified text can hinder ROCHESTER's ability to obtain necessary evidence (the confidential information itself) to enforce the NDA, impeding its ability to monitor compliance with the agreement's obligations. The law mandates full disclosure, while the modification could obstruct this process.

Next § 3101. Scope of disclosure. (a) Generally. There shall be full disclosure of all matter material and necessary in the prosecution or defense of an action, regardless of the burden of proof, by: (1) a party, or the officer, director, member, agent or employee of a party; (2) a person who possessed a cause of action or defense asserted in the action; (3) a person about to depart from the state, or without the state, or residing at a greater distance from the place of trial than one hundred miles, or so sick or infirm as to afford reasonable grounds of belief that he or she will not be able to attend the trial, or a person authorized to practice medicine, dentistry or podiatry who has provided medical, dental or podiatric care or diagnosis to the party demanding disclosure, or who has been retained by such party as an expert witness; and (4) any other person, upon notice stating the circumstances or reasons such disclosure is sought or required. (b) Privileged matter. Upon objection by a person entitled to assert the privilege, privileged matter shall not be obtainable. (c) Attorney's work product. The work product of an attorney shall not be obtainable. (d) Trial preparation. 1. Experts. (i) Upon request, each party shall identify each person whom the party expects to call as an expert witness at trial and shall disclose in reasonable detail the subject matter on which each expert is expected to testify, the subst
perturbation.justification: The original contract mandates the return of confidential information upon request, while the changed text makes this return discretionary by stating the company will 'consider' the request. This contradicts New York CPLR § 3101, which mandates 'full disclosure of all matter material and necessary in the prosecution or defense of an action'. The modified text's discretionary language obstructs the ability of ROCHESTER to obtain necessary information, thus hindering full disclosure as required by law. The scraped snippet directly supports this, as it states 'There shall be full disclosure of all matter material and necessary in the prosecution or defense of an action, regardless of the burden of proof'.

perturbation.original_text: 8. This Agreement shall be construed and enforced under the internal laws of the State of New York.
perturbation.changed_text: 8. This Agreement will generally be interpreted in accordance with the spirit of New York State law.
perturbation.explanation: The original text provides a clear choice of law, specifying that New York law governs the interpretation and enforcement of the agreement. The modified text replaces this with a vague reference to 'the spirit of New York State law,' which introduces significant ambiguity. The term 'spirit' is subjective and lacks legal precision, making it difficult to determine the applicable legal standards and creates a contradiction as legal contracts cannot be based on the spirit of a law but rather the law itself.
perturbation.law_explanation: New York GOL § 5-1401 allows parties to a contract to agree that New York law will govern their rights and duties, whether or not the contract bears a reasonable relation to New York. By replacing a clear choice of law with a vague reference to 'the spirit of New York State law,' the modified text undermines the enforceability of this provision. The original agreement provides for clear enforcement in New York, whereas the new verbiage undermines this right. The law allows for a clear designation, while the modified text creates uncertainty.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: New York General Obligations Law § 5-1401 (2022) - Choice of Law. :: 2022 New York Laws :: U.S. Codes and Statutes :: U.S. Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of the New York Laws 2024 N.Y. Laws 2023 N.Y. Laws 2022 N.Y. Laws (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2022 New York Laws GOB - General Obligations Article 5 - Creation, Definition and Enforcement of Contractual Obligations Title 14 - Enforceability of Clauses Respecting Choice of Law and Choice of Forum in Certain Transactions 5-1401 - Choice of Law. Universal Citation: NY Gen Oblig L § 5-1401 (2022) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Next § 5-1401. Choice of law. 1. The parties to any contract, agreement or undertaking, contingent or otherwise, in consideration of, or relating to any obligation arising out of a transaction covering in the aggregate not less than two hundred fifty thousand dollars, including a transaction otherwise covered by subsection (a) of section 1--301 of the uniform commercial code, may agree that the law of this state shall govern their rights and duties in whole or in part, whether or not such contract, agreement or undertaking bears a reasonable relation to this state. This section shall not apply to any contract, agreement or undertaking (a) for labor or personal services, (b) relating to any transaction for personal, family or household services, or (c) to the extent provided to the contrary in subsection (c) of section 1--301 of the uniform commercial code. 2. Nothing contained in this section shall be construed to limit or deny the enforcement of any provision respecting choice of law in any other contract, agreement or undertaking. perturbation.justification: The original contract specifies that it should be construed and enforced under the laws of New York. The changed text states that the agreement will generally be interpreted in accordance with the spirit of New York State law. This change contradicts the original agreement because it replaces a specific and enforceable legal standard with a vague and subjective concept. NY Gen Oblig L § 5-1401 allows parties to agree that New York law will govern their rights and duties, regardless of whether the contract has a reasonable relation to New York, thus allowing for the clear enforcement in New York as the original text states.

perturbation.original_text: 9. This Agreement represents the entire agreement of the parties relating to Confidential Information , and any corresponding patent rights, and any and all contemporaneous and prior oral and written understandings and agreements with respect thereto are superseded by this document.
perturbation.changed_text: 9. This Agreement generally aims to encompass the understandings of the parties relating to Confidential Information and associated patent rights, while acknowledging the potential existence of other related agreements.
perturbation.explanation: The original text is an integration clause, stating that the agreement is the *entire* agreement and supersedes all prior understandings. The changed text weakens this by saying the agreement 'generally aims to encompass' the understandings while 'acknowledging the potential existence of other related agreements'. This introduces ambiguity by suggesting that other agreements may exist and be relevant, undermining the purpose of the integration clause.
perturbation.law_explanation: New York GOL § 15-301 concerns written agreements that contain a provision to the effect that it cannot be changed orally. It sets standards for enforcing such provisions. By creating doubts about what is included in the agreement and adding the verbiage about other potential agreements, ROCHESTER now must prove the intent of the document as a sole agreement to ensure its proper enforcement. This ambiguity contradicts ROCHESTER's ability to defend the agreement from any potential claims.

Next § 15-301. When written agreement or other instrument cannot be changed by oral executory agreement, or discharged or terminated by oral executory agreement or oral consent or by oral notice. 1. A written agreement or other written instrument which contains a provision to the effect that it cannot be changed orally, cannot be changed by an executory agreement unless such executory agreement is in writing and signed by the party against whom enforcement of the change is sought or by his agent. 2. A written agreement or other written instrument which contains a provision to the effect that it cannot be terminated orally, cannot be discharged by an executory agreement unless such executory agreement is in writing and signed by the party against whom enforcement of the discharge is sought, or by his agent, and cannot be terminated by mutual consent unless such termination is effected by an executed accord and satisfaction other than the substitution of one executory contract for another, or is evidenced by a writing signed by the party against
perturbation.justification: The original text contains an integration clause stating that the written agreement is the entire agreement, superseding prior understandings. The changed text introduces ambiguity by suggesting the potential existence of other related agreements, thereby weakening the original integration clause. This weakening impacts the enforceability of the agreement as a whole, because it allows for the possibility that other agreements exist and are relevant. NY Gen Oblig L § 15-301 addresses written agreements that contain a provision stating it cannot be changed orally, setting standards for enforcing such provisions. By creating ambiguity about what's included in the agreement, ROCHESTER now has to prove the intent of the document as a sole agreement to ensure proper enforcement. This contradicts ROCHESTER's ability to defend the agreement from potential claims. The changed text undermines the purpose of the integration clause. The change introduces ambiguity and undermines the enforceability of the original integration clause, creating a legal contradiction by making it more difficult to defend the agreement as the sole agreement between the parties as defined in law.
